---
title: "What Schemas Have in Common"
id: sec-schemas-have-in-common
---

# What Schemas Have in Common {#sec-schemas-have-in-common}

## Schemas Are Graphs

A *directed graph* is a quadruple $(V, E, s, t)$: a set of vertices $V$, a set of edges $E$, and functions $s, t: E \to V$. A JSON Schema with an `object` containing a `name` of type `string` is a directed graph with two vertices and one edge:

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "name": { "type": "string" }
  }
}
```

$V = \{\texttt{object}, \texttt{string}\}$, $E = \{\texttt{name}\}$, $s(\texttt{name}) = \texttt{object}$, $t(\texttt{name}) = \texttt{string}$.

Schemas quickly outgrow simple directed graphs. Consider an ATProto Lexicon record with two string fields:

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "displayName": { "type": "string", "maxLength": 640 },
    "description": { "type": "string", "maxLength": 2560 }
  }
}
```

Both `displayName` and `description` are edges from `object` to `string`. A plain directed graph collapses them into one edge. We need a *multigraph*: a directed graph that allows parallel edges between the same pair of vertices.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-multigraph-atproto
%%| fig-cap: "An ATProto profile schema as a directed multigraph. Two parallel edges from `object` to `string`, distinguished by label and constraint."
graph LR
    Object((object))
    Str((string))

    Object -- "displayName<br/>maxLength: 640" --> Str
    Object -- "description<br/>maxLength: 2560" --> Str
```

The moment you have two fields of the same type (which is nearly always), you need at least a multigraph. Most schema languages live here.

Some languages need even more. A SQL *foreign key* connects four things at once: source table, source column, referenced table, referenced column.

```sql
ALTER TABLE posts
    ADD CONSTRAINT fk_posts_author
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES authors (id);
```

You can't represent that as a binary edge without losing information. *Hypergraphs* handle it: they allow edges (called hyperedges) that connect an ordered sequence of vertices, not just two.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-hypergraph-sql
%%| fig-cap: "A SQL foreign key as a hyperedge connecting four entities."
graph TB
    subgraph Posts ["posts (table)"]
        P_id["id : BIGINT PK"]
        P_author["author_id : BIGINT"]
    end

    subgraph Authors ["authors (table)"]
        A_id["id : BIGINT PK"]
    end

    FK{{"fk_posts_author"}}

    FK -. "source table" .-> Posts
    FK -. "source column" .-> P_author
    FK -. "referenced table" .-> Authors
    FK -. "referenced column" .-> A_id

    style FK fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

Composite unique constraints, Protobuf `oneof` declarations, and GraphQL union types are all multi-ary relationships that benefit from hypergraph representation.

### GitHub's GraphQL Schema as a Graph

Consider a fragment of GitHub's GraphQL schema:

```graphql
type Repository {
  name: String!
  description: String
  issues(first: Int): IssueConnection!
  pullRequests(first: Int): PullRequestConnection!
  owner: RepositoryOwner!
}

type Issue {
  title: String!
  body: String!
  author: Actor
}
```

As a multigraph: `Repository`, `Issue`, `String`, `IssueConnection`, `PullRequestConnection`, `RepositoryOwner`, `Int`, `Actor` are vertices. Each field is an edge. `Repository` has five outgoing edges, two of which (`name` and `description`) both target `String`: parallel edges, requiring multigraph structure. The entire GitHub GraphQL schema is a multigraph with hundreds of vertices and thousands of edges, all following the same pattern: types as vertices, fields as edges.

### Constraints Decorate the Graph

Schemas carry more than connectivity. They have *constraints*: `maxLength`, `NOT NULL`, `DEFAULT`, `pattern`, `minimum`, `maxItems`. Constraints don't change the graph structure; they annotate it. A `maxLength` on a string property attaches metadata to an existing edge. It doesn't add new vertices or edges.

Different schema languages support different constraints:

| Constraint        | JSON Schema | Protobuf      | SQL DDL       | GraphQL   | ATProto Lexicon |
|-------------------|-------------|---------------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| maxLength         | yes         | no            | VARCHAR(n)    | no        | yes             |
| pattern (regex)   | yes         | no            | CHECK         | no        | no              |
| minimum / maximum | yes         | no            | CHECK         | no        | yes             |
| NOT NULL          | required[]  | (default set) | NOT NULL      | `!`       | required[]      |
| DEFAULT           | default     | default value | DEFAULT       | no        | default         |
| UNIQUE            | no          | no            | UNIQUE        | no        | no              |
| PRIMARY KEY       | no          | no            | PRIMARY KEY   | no        | no              |

The constraint vocabulary varies wildly. The mechanism (annotations on a graph) is uniform.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Question

An ATProto Lexicon has three string fields with different `maxLength` constraints. Draw the multigraph. Is `maxLength` a vertex, an edge, or something else?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Answer

The multigraph has three vertices (`object`, `string`, `string`) and two parallel edges from `object` to `string` (one for `displayName`, one for `description`). `maxLength` is neither a vertex nor an edge; it is a *constraint annotation* on a vertex. It decorates the `string` vertex with additional metadata. This is why we need a constraint layer on top of the graph structure.
:::

We have been describing graph structures informally. To make "schemas are graphs with constraints" precise enough for an engine to act on, we need a formal language for describing structure itself.

## Theories

A *theory* $\mathbb{T} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{O}, \mathcal{E})$ consists of three components:

- **Sorts** $\mathcal{S}$: the types of entities. In a graph theory, $\mathcal{S} = \{\text{Vertex}, \text{Edge}\}$. In a SQL schema theory, $\mathcal{S}$ includes `Table`, `Column`, `ForeignKey`, and more. (panproto uses "sort" rather than "type" to avoid confusion with schema-level types like `string` or `integer`.)

- **Operations** $\mathcal{O}$: functions connecting sorts. $\text{src}: \text{Edge} \to \text{Vertex}$ says "every edge has a source vertex." Operations define the relationships between sorts.

- **Equations** $\mathcal{E}$: invariants that operations must satisfy. In SQL, $\text{table}(\text{srcColumn}(fk)) = \text{srcTable}(fk)$ says: take a foreign key, get its source column, ask which table that column belongs to; you get the same answer as asking the foreign key directly for its source table.

A concrete schema that provides actual sets and functions satisfying a theory is called a *model*. The relationship is the same as between a TypeScript interface and a class implementing it: the theory says what must exist and what laws hold; the model fills in the specifics.

### The Directed Graph Theory

The simplest useful theory:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{\text{Vertex}, \text{Edge}\} \quad \mathcal{O} = \{\text{src}: \text{Edge} \to \text{Vertex},\; \text{tgt}: \text{Edge} \to \text{Vertex}\} \quad \mathcal{E} = \emptyset$$

A model of this theory is a specific graph. An ATProto schema, viewed as a multigraph, is a model:

- $\text{Vertex} = \{\texttt{object}, \texttt{string}, \texttt{datetime}, \texttt{array}, \texttt{ref}\}$
- $\text{Edge} = \{\texttt{text}, \texttt{createdAt}, \texttt{tags}, \texttt{reply}\}$
- $s(\texttt{text}) = \texttt{object}$, $t(\texttt{text}) = \texttt{string}$
- $s(\texttt{createdAt}) = \texttt{object}$, $t(\texttt{createdAt}) = \texttt{datetime}$

The schema *is* the model.

### The Monoid: Simplest Theory with Equations

A monoid is a set with an associative binary operation and an identity element. String concatenation is a monoid. `Array.prototype.reduce` with an initial value uses a monoid.

$$\mathcal{S} = \{\text{Carrier}\}$$

$$\mathcal{O} = \{\text{mul}: \text{Carrier} \times \text{Carrier} \to \text{Carrier},\; \text{unit}: () \to \text{Carrier}\}$$

$$\mathcal{E} = \begin{cases} \text{mul}(\text{mul}(a, b), c) = \text{mul}(a, \text{mul}(b, c)) & \text{(associativity)} \\ \text{mul}(\text{unit}(), a) = a & \text{(left identity)} \\ \text{mul}(a, \text{unit}()) = a & \text{(right identity)} \end{cases}$$

Three models: integers under addition ($\text{unit} = 0$), strings under concatenation ($\text{unit} = \texttt{""}$), booleans under AND ($\text{unit} = \text{true}$). Different concrete sets and functions, same theory.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Question

Write out the theory for a *reflexive graph* (a directed graph where every vertex has a distinguished self-loop). How many sorts, operations, and equations?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Answer

Two sorts (`Vertex`, `Edge`), three operations (`src: Edge → Vertex`, `tgt: Edge → Vertex`, `id: Vertex → Edge`), and two equations (`src(id(v)) = v`, `tgt(id(v)) = v`). The `id` operation assigns each vertex a distinguished self-loop, and the equations ensure that self-loop starts and ends at the same vertex.
:::

The theories so far treat all vertices uniformly. But real constraints are not uniform: `maxLength` makes sense on strings, not integers. We need sorts that depend on context.

## Dependent Sorts

Consider modeling constraints as a single flat sort:

```
Constraint = { maxLength, pattern, minimum, maximum, NOT_NULL, CHECK, ... }
```

Now you need a runtime check: "is this constraint valid for this vertex?" `maxLength` only makes sense on string-typed vertices. `CHECK` only makes sense on SQL columns. `maxGraphemes` only makes sense on ATProto string properties. If you attach `maxLength` to an integer vertex, you have a malformed schema, but nothing in the theory prevents it.

The problem: the *set of valid constraints depends on which vertex you're constraining*. String vertices accept `maxLength` and `pattern`. Integer vertices accept `minimum` and `maximum`. The constraint vocabulary is not one set; it's a family of sets, indexed by vertex.

A *Generalized Algebraic Theory* (GAT) expresses this directly with *dependent sorts*: sorts parameterized by terms of other sorts.^[The foundational treatment of GATs is due to @cartmell1986.]

- $\text{Vertex}$: ordinary sort (arity 0)
- $\text{Edge}$: ordinary sort (arity 0)
- $\text{Constraint}(v: \text{Vertex})$: dependent sort (arity 1), a *different* set of constraints for each vertex $v$

$\text{Constraint}(\text{stringVertex}) = \{\text{maxLength}, \text{pattern}\}$. $\text{Constraint}(\text{intVertex}) = \{\text{minimum}, \text{maximum}\}$. These are genuinely different sets. A model that tries to put `maxLength` in $\text{Constraint}(\text{intVertex})$ is not a valid model; the types don't match.

The alternative (a flat `Constraint` sort with a runtime `appliesTo` check) is to dependent sorts what TypeScript's `any` is to precise types. Both let you write correct programs, but dependent sorts catch constraint errors structurally rather than at runtime.

With sorts, operations, equations, and dependent sorts in hand, we can write down complete theories for real schema languages.

## Theories for Real Schema Languages

Here are the theories for three schema languages.

### JSON Schema

**Sorts:** `SchemaNode`, `Property`, $\text{Constraint}(s: \text{SchemaNode})$

**Operations:**

- $\text{owner}: \text{Property} \to \text{SchemaNode}$ (the object owning this property)
- $\text{target}: \text{Property} \to \text{SchemaNode}$ (the property's type)
- $\text{name}: \text{Property} \to \text{String}$
- $\text{required}: \text{Property} \to \text{Bool}$
- $\text{schemaType}: \text{SchemaNode} \to \text{TypeTag}$
- $\text{maxLength}: \text{Constraint}(\text{stringNode}) \to \mathbb{N}$
- $\text{pattern}: \text{Constraint}(\text{stringNode}) \to \text{Regex}$
- $\text{minimum}: \text{Constraint}(\text{numericNode}) \to \mathbb{R}$

**Equations:** $\text{schemaType}(\text{owner}(p)) = \texttt{object}$ (properties belong to objects).

### SQL DDL

**Sorts:** `Table`, `Column`, `ForeignKey`, $\text{Constraint}(c: \text{Column})$

**Operations:**

- $\text{table}: \text{Column} \to \text{Table}$
- $\text{colType}: \text{Column} \to \text{DataType}$
- $\text{srcTable}: \text{ForeignKey} \to \text{Table}$
- $\text{srcColumn}: \text{ForeignKey} \to \text{Column}$
- $\text{refTable}: \text{ForeignKey} \to \text{Table}$
- $\text{refColumn}: \text{ForeignKey} \to \text{Column}$
- $\text{notNull}: \text{Column} \to \text{Bool}$
- $\text{defaultVal}: \text{Column} \to \text{Option}\langle\text{Expr}\rangle$

**Equations:**

- $\text{table}(\text{srcColumn}(fk)) = \text{srcTable}(fk)$ (the source column belongs to the source table)
- $\text{table}(\text{refColumn}(fk)) = \text{refTable}(fk)$ (the referenced column belongs to the referenced table)

These equations encode structural invariants that every SQL database enforces. panproto can verify schema well-formedness before generating any SQL.

### Protobuf

**Sorts:** `Message`, `Field`, `EnumType`, `EnumValue`

**Operations:**

- $\text{message}: \text{Field} \to \text{Message}$
- $\text{fieldType}: \text{Field} \to \text{TypeRef}$
- $\text{fieldNumber}: \text{Field} \to \mathbb{N}$ (the wire format number)
- $\text{enumType}: \text{EnumValue} \to \text{EnumType}$
- $\text{enumNumber}: \text{EnumValue} \to \mathbb{Z}$

**Equations:** For distinct fields $f_1, f_2$ in the same message, $\text{fieldNumber}(f_1) \neq \text{fieldNumber}(f_2)$. Field numbers are unique within a message.

The uniqueness equation is what makes Protobuf's wire format work. It is a Protobuf-specific structural law, not shared by JSON Schema or SQL.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Question

The SQL theory has two equations, both of the form $\text{table}(\text{column}(fk)) = \text{directAccessor}(fk)$. Protobuf has a disequality constraint on field numbers. JSON Schema has one equation constraining `owner` to be of type `object`. What does the *presence* of equations in a theory tell you about the structural complexity of the schema language? What would a theory with no equations imply?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Answer

Equations encode structural invariants that the schema language enforces. A theory with equations (like SQL's foreign key consistency) describes a language where certain relationships between elements are *guaranteed*, not merely possible. A theory with no equations (like the basic directed graph theory) imposes no invariants beyond connectivity: any assignment of sets and functions is a valid model. More equations means the language is more opinionated about what constitutes a well-formed schema.
:::

These mathematical definitions are not just notation; they are the data structures panproto operates on.

## The Rust Implementation

panproto represents theories as data structures: values you construct, inspect, and pass to algorithms.

### Sorts

```{.rust include="../../crates/panproto-gat/src/sort.rs" start-line=52 end-line=58}
```

A `SortParam` captures the name and sort of each parameter in a dependent sort. For $\text{Constraint}(v: \text{Vertex})$, there's one `SortParam` with name `"v"` and a sort reference pointing to `Vertex`.

```{.rust include="../../crates/panproto-gat/src/sort.rs" start-line=72 end-line=81}
```

A `Sort` has a name and a list of parameters. For `Vertex` and `Edge`, the parameter list is empty. For $\text{Constraint}(v: \text{Vertex})$, it contains one entry.

### Operations

```{.rust include="../../crates/panproto-gat/src/op.rs" start-line=15 end-line=23}
```

An `Operation` has a name, input sorts, and an output sort. $\text{src}: \text{Edge} \to \text{Vertex}$ becomes an `Operation` with one input of sort `Edge` and output sort `Vertex`.

### Theories

```{.rust include="../../crates/panproto-gat/src/theory.rs" start-line=50 end-line=76}
```

The `Theory` struct holds sorts, operations, and equations. When panproto diffs two schemas, plans a migration, or generates code, it consults the theory to determine the structural shape it's working with.

Here's the monoid theory constructed as an actual Rust test from the panproto-gat crate:

```{.rust include="../../crates/panproto-gat/src/theory.rs" start-line=426 end-line=478}
```

The code mirrors the mathematical specification: sorts declared first, then operations with their input and output sorts, then equations as pairs of terms that must be equal.

### Machine-Checked Equations

panproto type-checks every equation when you define a theory. Both sides must have the same sort, and every operation must be applied to arguments of the correct sort. An equation claiming a `Vertex`-sorted term equals an `Edge`-sorted term is rejected with a clear error message.

The CLI checks equations automatically. `schema add` verifies equations as part of staging; `schema commit` re-verifies before creating the commit. You can also check explicitly:

```bash
schema verify my-theory.json
```

When you compose theories via colimit (structured merge: gluing two theories together while identifying their shared parts), the equations from each component are re-checked in the combined theory.

For implementation details, see the [GAT engine internals](https://panproto.dev/dev-guide/chapters/06-gat-engine.html).^[The foundational framework: @spivak2012 showed database schemas can be treated as categories with migration computed by composing maps between them. panproto generalizes this from relational databases to arbitrary schema languages. The GAT framework [@cartmell1986] adds dependent sorts, implemented via an equivalence with Essentially Algebraic Theories [@adamek1994]. The computational architecture adapts [GATlab](https://algebraicjulia.github.io/GATlab.jl/) [@lynch2024] to Rust. See [Appendix A](../appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd) for the full mathematical treatment.]
